MASTER CALENDAR JURY
Off Calendar Feb-06-2026 Set For Trial On Feb-01-27 At 09:30 A.M. In Dept 206

OBJECTION HEARING CALENDAR
Off Calendar Jan-13-2026 Off Calendar

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Dec-29-2025 Set For Trial On Nov-09-26 At 09:30 A.M. In Dept 206

DEMURRER to COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Nov-20-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT CHARLES SCHWAB & CO., INC'S DEMURRER to COMPLAINT.

Defendant Charles Schwab & Co., Inc.'s demurrer to plaintiff Noora Barakat's verified complaint (sixth, seventh, and eighth causes of action) is overruled.

A demurrer tests whether the challenged cause of action necessarily fails to state a claim for relief. (Blank v. Kirwan (1985) 39 Cal.3d 311, 318.) In assessing whether the complaint states a cause of action, the court accepts all properly pleaded material facts, but not contentions, deductions, or conclusions of fact or law. (Minton v. Dignity Health (2019) 39 Cal.App.5th 1155, 1161.) The court liberally construes the complaint pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure, section 452.

Barakat asserts causes of action for failure to provide accurate itemized wage statements, failure to pay vested vacation wages, and waiting time penalties. Her allegations assert that Schwab violated statutory obligations giving rise to each cause of action but do not supply particularized facts. A complaint must contain "[a] statement of the facts constituting the cause of action, in ordinary and concise language." (Code Civ. Proc., section 425.10, subd. (a)(1).) "[T]he complaint ordinarily is sufficient if it alleges ultimate rather than evidentiary facts." (Doe v. City of Los Angeles (2007) 42 Cal.4th 531, 550.)

Sometimes there is no difference between an ultimate fact and a legal conclusion, such as when a pleading alleges that someone owned a piece of property. The court concludes here that Barakat's pleading of ultimate facts is sufficient to satisfy California's notice-pleading requirement for non-fraud claims. Schwab relies on federal district court cases applying a 12(b)(6) standard that is intended to weed out nonmeritorious claims. California has a different standard: unlike in federal court, "[t]he pleading of fraud . . . is . . . the last remaining habitat of the common law notion that a complaint should be sufficiently specific that the court can weed out nonmeritorious actions on the basis of the pleadings." (Committee On Children's Television, Inc. v. General Foods Corp. (1983) 35 Cal.3d 197, 216-217.) Barakat has not pled fraud claims.

Schwab also relies on summary judgment cases, which do not prescribe the pleading standard, and on Green v. Grimes-Stassforth Stationery Co. (1940) 39 Cal.App.2d 52, 56 ["Where a party relies for recovery upon a purely statutory liability it is indispensable that he plead facts demonstrating his right to recover under the statute. The complaint must plead every fact which is essential to the cause of action under the statute."]. Green was narrowed in G.H.I.I. v. MTS, Inc. (1983) 147 Cal.App.3d 256, 272. The court also observes that Barakat's pleading is consistent with the particularity in practice guide model forms. (See, e.g., 14 Am. Jur. Pl. & Pr. Forms Independent Contractors sec. 5.)

Order is signed.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: W. Trupek; Not Reported (301/CVA)
